chapters.title: Computing power plays a central role in Bitcoin formation
chapters.text:
Twenty-five years ago, the term “cryptocurrency” could have been part of the lexicon of some utopian science-fiction book. Unknown and exotic, it seemed impossible to implement in society given the tight grip fiat currency kept over the world. Against all odds, digital money survived. Not only did it become a thing, but it also revealed some defects that the classic financial system contained.

Banks and financial institutions take the liberty of regulating the money flow. They establish exchange rates, charge fees for the operations, scrutinize people’s accounts for fraudulent activities, and have unlimited access to the funds. In this world of banking dictatorship, cryptocurrency, namely Bitcoin, is like a breath of fresh air.

Cryptocurrency enables people to take full ownership of their money and control it whichever way they want.

At the core of Bitcoin lies the code — a complex equation that is hard to break. When people join forces and contribute their computers to the process, the code runs, and the coins emerge. That makes Bitcoin stand out — it has become a global phenomenon, erasing borders between countries and uniting humans worldwide. Furthermore, one may change the software if most participants agree, which makes the system fair and democratic.

Decentralization is the main principle of cryptocurrency — there are no third parties or mediators. There is no need to trust the money to the institutions that might become insolvent or declare bankruptcy one day. All transactions stay secret from the authorities, and the only way to get hold of coins is to provide the private key. Those who disapprove of banks’ supremacy will find this approach very appealing — it gives freedom of action, and no one is watching you.This summary will be a great starting point for those willing to dive into the digital currency world and understand the crux of Bitcoin. You will see money in a broader and utterly new context.

chapters.title: The dawn of Bitcoin
chapters.text:
The concept of Bitcoin hasn’t emerged out of thin air. A whole set of ideas and projects preceded this invention. The technological realm’s finest minds gathered on online forums to brainstorm how to make the world a better place. One such group was Cypherpunks, who, among other pressing problems, also discussed the issue of money. They acknowledged that current financial institutions could not guarantee the security of personal data. By giving away details, the customer became vulnerable to fraud.

The pioneer of money digitalization was David Chaum. In his work, he adopted encryption elements — the public and the private keys. The former is a combination of letters and numbers that indicates a specific path. One may share this message with anyone worldwide — it means nothing without a private key. The latter must be a secret that only the owner should know.

Based on this knowledge, David Chaum developed DigiCash. This start-up company enabled people to manage their money without providing personal information. Though genuinely novel, the plan suffered from one major drawback that undermined its potentia l — customers still needed to confirm their data with David’s company, which imitated the banking system. The company went bankrupt; this precedent did not discourage other risk-takers from trying their luck.

Another encryption enthusiast, Adam Back, presented his vision of virtual currency based on the hash function. The principle of a hash function is straightforward — it contains mathematical equations that a particular computer needs to solve. Once the solution is satisfactory, the system grants a user the reward — hashcash.

The hash function used in cryptocurrencies guarantees the security.

Back’s innovation had several flaws, namely the impossibility of using one hashcash continually. This imperfection inspired other Cypherpunks to ponder alternatives with reusable currency. Nick Szabo presented the project ‘bit gold,’ whereas Wei Dai proposed ‘b-money.’ Unfortunately, these concepts didn’t stand a chance, but they shook up the community and gave rise to Bitcoin.

The experiments also suffered from a more fundamental difficulty, which was the issue of getting people to use and value these new digital tokens. ~ Nathaniel Popper

Nathaniel Popper

chapters.title: The mystical figure behind Bitcoin’s establishment
chapters.text:
Satoshi Nakamoto suggested Bitcoin — the upgraded version of digital currency. These days, no one knows the real identity of the software’s creator, which breeds many theories and arguments. Some claim that his name has nothing to do with his nationality and Satoshi Nakamoto is the alias. Others speculate that Bitcoin is the brainchild of a group of developers who couldn’t pitch this idea to their company and decided to publish it online to the whole world. Still, no trace could lead to this personality, as he disappeared from the Internet shortly after Bitcoin took off. Undoubtedly, this mystery adds to the unique charm of Bitcoin.

It all started when Satoshi distributed his constructive ideas in a PDF file throughout the Cypherpunk community. Back then, tech geeks attached little value to this document. The concept seemed unfeasible to them, and no one was willing to try it except for Hal Finney, who was the second person (after Satoshi) to make a trial run on this digital currency. He became the first recipient of a Bitcoin transaction in the world. The document mentioned earlier became a Bitcoin white paper that now serves as a handbook to anyone interested in this subject.

Which exact improvements did Nakamoto carry out that made his project so durable? First and foremost, Bitcoin enabled the system of decentralization. The third-party figure was a significant flaw in Digicash, but with Satoshi’s enhancements, it was gone.

Every Bitcoin transaction is recorded and overseen by users' computers, also ensuring its regulation.

With public and private keys, customers could initialize a transaction around the globe. Before finalizing it, computers on the network need to check the system and confirm it. If approved, the transaction joins the general list, a blockchain. Similarly to Back’s innovation, the computers participate in a particular competition, and the winner receives a batch of coins. The system generally allows only 21 million Bitcoins to be programmed, which makes this currency a valuable asset. However ground-breaking the concept of Bitcoin was, it was still in the infant stage of development. It lacked one more thing — people’s faith that this currency was the future.

chapters.title: Bitcoin’s privacy fuled its use in the dark markets
chapters.text:
Bitcoin strongly appealed to Ross Ulbricht, a young man from Texas, mainly because it corresponded to his political and moral beliefs. Ross was an ardent supporter of libertarianism — he believed that the government only hinders people from relishing life; it imposes the boundaries society must follow. With digital currency, the chances of authorities taking over decrease. Eventually, Ross came up with the project that reflected his eagerness to rebel — Silk Road.

Silk Road was an online platform created to distribute the products that are hard to get — drugs and other illegal goods. Ross mainly utilized two protection tools — Tor to cover the IP location and Bitcoin as the means of payment, perfect for hiding clients’ identities. These features made this dark market alluring to illegal vendors and almost impossible to crack for the Federal Bureau. The first products were the drugs Ross created himself — he set up a laboratory in his house.

The founder of the Silk Road wanted to manifest his high ideals and win the battle with the state.

After launching the Silk Road, the prices of Bitcoin rocketed. The virtual currency finally gained recognition — it became real money with actual value. A substantial share of Bitcoin money circulated on the website of Ross Ulbricht. More alarming for Bitcoin followers was that people exploited coins but not for noble purposes. The fraudulent activity could draw the government’s attention and bring discredit to the whole concept of cryptocurrency.

Indeed, the Silk Road was too massive for the authorities to leave unnoticed, and the FBI kept an eye on it for a long time. The undercover cops were constantly sneaking around the website, making purchases, and trying to get a lead on the mastermind behind one of the most prominent dark markets. Eventually, Ross Ulbricht let his guard down and was held captive. Luckily for many Bitcoin enthusiasts, the fall of the Silk Road didn’t have any repercussions on digital currency. Despite such a gloomy background, Bitcoin carried on.

Did you know? In 2013, Silk Road’s online website had almost 1 million accounts registered.

chapters.title: Security is one of the pillars of digital money
chapters.text:
In the world of computer nerds, Bitcoin became a thing once the online journal Slashdot published an article on this subject. This occasion boosted downloading software worldwide and motivated some individuals to design currency exchange websites. Such a man was Jed McCaleb, the creator of Mt. Gox. Jed hated the idea of the government holding the reins of everything — he wished for individual freedom, and Bitcoin was a pure representation of libertarianism.

The idea behind Mt. Gox was uncomplicated — Jed collected customers’ money on his Paypal account and then exchanged them. One could trade his funds anytime, which was a significant advantage. However, given McCaleb’s little competence in security technology, handling a business like that involved a lot of risks, and such a system was a dainty morsel for hackers. Jed could not provide insurance for his stored funds — he was playing with fire.

Indeed, Mt. Gox became a victim of numerous hacker attacks, which induced Jed to think about how to avoid such a hazardous situation. He managed to sell his company to a young entrepreneur from France based in Japan — Mark Karpeles. Mark was an interesting choice to run a business. By nature, he was somewhat reluctant and preferred relying only on himself — such an attitude was disastrous for the company that was one of the leading Bitcoin exchanges in the world.

Any organization that works with money requires security.

Security issues led to the collapse of Mt. Gox. In 2014, the customers became anxious they couldn’t make a withdrawal of their funds; the company was under fierce criticism. The issue led to further investigation, and the reveal of the tragic news — almost 750 000 coins disappeared from the Mt. Gox system irreversibly. Mark Karpeles was unaware of stealing such money; this process took place for years — someone managed to change the transaction codes and crack the system.

The destiny of Mt. Gox was definite — the company went bankrupt. Mark Karpeles

found fault with the Bitcoin code created by Satoshi. Luckily for digital money fans, this accident didn’t undermine cryptocurrency’s influence worldwide. The Bitcoin software had a flaw, but other companies had already acknowledged it and made their way around it, which helped them survive and go on with the transactions.

chapters.title: BitInstant resolved the issue people had with Mt. Gox
chapters.text:
Mt. Gox was one of the leading players in the Bitcoin game. People would use this service despite some obvious flaws — it was too slow. Overseas transactions would take a lot of time to get processed, and to add Mark’s apathy toward some urgent matters — these factors made some people dwell on the new opportunities for the market. Such a product was BitInstant, created by Charlie Shrem and Gareth Nelson.

The peculiarity of the project was the ability to eliminate international transactions and make the process of Bitcoin trade more accessible and faster. Customers didn’t have anything to worry about; BitInstant cooperated with slow Mt. Gox.

BitInstant owed its success to Charlie Shrem — a charismatic and confident young man from Brooklyn. Charlie didn’t have an aptitude for management, which had a detrimental effect on the organization. He, however, could understand human nature and possessed enormous power of persuasion. While Charlie was working on attracting potential investors, Gareth Nelson was responsible for the technical part. BitInstant was profitable, and many entrepreneurs pushed forward to get on this boat. Charlie Shrem was also a member of the Bitcoin Foundation, which specializes in promoting this cryptocurrency and advertising its benefits.

Charlie Shrem’s connection with the Silk Road was the only point in question. Charlie was aware of the risks and proceeded to supply people with Bitcoin to make purchases on the Silk Road.

Being involved in the dark market and handling a Bitcoin business projected a negative image of cryptocurrency.

Charlie’s company was proliferating as an exchange enterprise. If the authorities found out about the dubious connection with Silk Road, this fact could cause severe reputational damage. In the meantime, some banks rejected BitInstant as their client, which complicated the workflow. Eventually, handling such a company became almost unbearable, coinciding with Charlie facing charges for his illegal involvement.

The notion of Bitcoiners around the world sitting on their private keys and waiting to become rich begged the question of the intrinsic value of these digital files. ~ Nathaniel Popper

Nathaniel Popper

chapters.title: Spreading Bitcoin throughout Silicon Valley
chapters.text:
It may seem that nothing good may come from Bitcoin. Currency used on dark markets and lost funds from the exchange companies — society started associating this cryptocurrency with gruesome things, like fraud and delusion. Some endeavors were truly unfortunate; most people who took part in them misunderstood the primary purpose of digital money and overlooked the philosophy of Satoshi Nakamoto. Still, some enthusiasts put their moral principles first and wanted to utilize cryptocurrency for higher goals.

The person who deemed Bitcoin as a real lifesaver was Wences Casares. As a kid, he grew up in Argentina and observed his country’s financial situation. The crisis and rampant inflation crushed the Argentinian currency — the peso and made it almost priceless. There was no point in collecting such money; it had no value in the long run. Argentinians exchanged pesos for dollars, a more stable currency, but the government thoroughly scrutinized this process.

Wences was already a successful businessman when he discovered Bitcoin. He owned a few start-ups and didn’t have a strong need for investments. Still, whenever he visited his homeland, he felt the urge to make a difference. Wences wanted to promote a different vision of money.

The ledger, not the banknotes or gold pieces, holds value and power.

Each item in the ledger automatically became a hot property, making Bitcoin a perfect option for Wences. He believed that this digital money would facilitate the lives of Argentine citizens.

Wences became the mastermind behind Xapo — a financial institution providing secure Bitcoin storage. Previous experience with Mt. Gox displayed the necessity of a particular vault, a safe place hackers and fraudulent organizations cannot violate. This start-up was a significant improvement and added to the prestige and credibility of Bitcoin. Wences Casares proved himself to be a staunch supporter of digital currency. He was restless in his preaching and helped the Bitcoin community gain many followers from Silicon Valley, along with their investments. Most importantly, Wences managed to get Bill Gates into the Bitcoin movement.

Did you know? Wences hired a pair of hackers to check if the Bitcoin code was easy to break. At the end of the experiment, they concluded that it was uncrackable.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Undoubtedly, crypto currency revolutionized the world of finances. Despite all the stereotypes and serious misgivings, digital money, particularly Bitcoin, is kept afloat. Not only does this virtual currency circulate, but it is also one of the most high-priced possessions in the world. Bitcoin managed to destroy the accepted image of money and bring a new perspective — people’s faith set value to things. Dollar bills are just paper, and digital coins are code-generated combinations. Still, people frequently chase these assets by participating in trades, investing in cryptocurrency companies, and climbing the corporate ladder.

The emergence of Bitcoin also revealed how dependent people are on financial institutions. It is common practice to entrust funds to the banks; all transactions go through payment providers that charge fees and set limitations on how to spend money. Cryptocurrency is all about freedom and independence, no hands tied. Storing money is your responsibility; you will find it undemanding if you are conscientious. With Bitcoin, your funds are your private matter, and no official institution can get access to it.

Try this


• Reinvent your attitude towards digital currency and money in general. Progress is only possible when people leave their comfort zone and let go of things they have stuck to for a long time.
• Be less skeptical toward any new invention in the financial sphere. As history shows, those who believed in Bitcoin at its earliest stage do not regret it — they are one of the wealthiest people in the world.
• Research digital money, namely Bitcoin, Ethereum, and Litecoin — you might find the concept engaging and valuable.
